approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3 m^3)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all possible subrectangles by fixing top-left and bottom-right corners, compute sum directly, and track maximum. Use it for small matrices or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Rectangle in 2D Matrix (Brute Force O(n³m³))
ladders.desc: Find the maximum sum sub-rectangle in a 2D matrix by checking all possible rectangles.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the maximum sum rectangle problem by systematically examining every possible sub-rectangle in the matrix. The intuition is straightforward: if we check every rectangle, we're guaranteed to find the maximum sum rectangle. We achieve this by fixing all possible top-left corners (r1, c1) and bottom-right corners (r2, c2), then computing the sum of elements within that rectangle. The algorithm iterates through four nested loops: two for rows (top and bottom boundaries) and two for columns (left and right boundaries). For each rectangle defined by these boundaries, we compute the sum by iterating through all elements within it using two more nested loops. We maintain a variable to track the maximum sum encountered. The time complexity is O(n³m³) because we have six nested loops: four for boundaries and two for sum computation. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach serves as a baseline for understanding the problem and is practical only for very small matrices (typically n,m ≤ 10). Common mistakes include incorrect boundary conditions (off-by-one errors), forgetting to handle negative numbers properly (the maximum sum could be negative if all elements are negative), and inefficient implementation that recalculates sums unnecessarily. The connection to more efficient approaches is that this brute force method establishes the problem's fundamental structure, which can then be optimized using techniques like prefix sums or Kadane's algorithm in 2D.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Sum of Any Rectangle Given Boundaries
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of all elements within a rectangular region defined by top-left and bottom-right coordinates.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational step in the brute force approach. Given a matrix and the coordinates of a rectangle (top-left row r1, top-left column c1, bottom-right row r2, bottom-right column c2), we need to sum all elements within that inclusive rectangle. The intuition is simple: we need to visit every cell in the rectangular region and accumulate their values. The algorithm uses two nested loops: an outer loop iterates through rows from r1 to r2 (inclusive), and an inner loop iterates through columns from c1 to c2 (inclusive). For each cell (i, j), we add matrix[i][j] to a running total. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O((r2-r1+1)*(c2-c1+1)), which in the worst case is O(n*m) when the rectangle covers the entire matrix. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for every possible rectangle combination. Common mistakes include incorrect boundary handling (using exclusive instead of inclusive ranges), assuming 1-based indexing when the matrix uses 0-based indexing, and not initializing the sum variable to 0. This sub-routine is independent and can be tested separately with fixed rectangle boundaries.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Possible Rectangle Boundaries
ladders.desc: Generate all valid combinations of top-left and bottom-right corners to define every possible sub-rectangle.
ladders.explanation: This is the central logic that drives the brute force approach. The insight is that any rectangle in a 2D matrix can be uniquely defined by its top-left corner (r1, c1) and bottom-right corner (r2, c2), where r1 ≤ r2 and c1 ≤ c2. To cover all rectangles, we need to iterate through all possible pairs of rows for the top and bottom boundaries, and all possible pairs of columns for the left and right boundaries. The algorithm uses four nested loops: the outer two loops iterate over all possible top rows (r1 from 0 to n-1) and bottom rows (r2 from r1 to n-1), ensuring the bottom row is at or below the top row. Similarly, the inner two loops iterate over all possible left columns (c1 from 0 to m-1) and right columns (c2 from c1 to m-1). This generates every valid rectangle exactly once. The time complexity for this iteration alone is O(n²m²), as there are O(n²) row pairs and O(m²) column pairs. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the rectangle boundaries, which are then passed to the sum computation sub-routine. Common mistakes include incorrect loop ranges (e.g., allowing r2 < r1), double-counting rectangles, or using inefficient iteration patterns. This logic is independent and can be tested by counting the number of rectangles generated for a given matrix size.

ladders.title: Building Block — Access and Iterate Through 2D Array Elements
ladders.desc: Navigate through a 2D matrix using nested loops and access individual elements by row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to working with 2D arrays. A 2D matrix is typically represented as a list of lists, where the outer list contains rows and each inner list contains column elements for that row. To access an element, we use two indices: matrix[row][column], where row is the index of the outer list (0-based) and column is the index within that inner list. Iteration through all elements requires two nested loops: an outer loop over rows (i from 0 to n-1) and an inner loop over columns (j from 0 to m-1). The intuition is that we systematically visit each cell in row-major order (left to right, top to bottom). Time complexity for visiting all elements is O(n*m). Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself. This feeds into the core logic by enabling the iteration over rectangle boundaries and the sum computation within rectangles. Common mistakes include confusing row and column indices, accessing out-of-bounds indices, incorrect loop ranges (off-by-one errors), and misunderstanding the matrix representation. This building block is essential for any 2D array manipulation and can be practiced independently by tasks like printing the matrix or computing its total sum.

examples.trace:
Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0 → matrix[0][0]=1, j=1 → matrix[0][1]=2
Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=0 → matrix[1][0]=3, j=1 → matrix[1][1]=4
Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0 → matrix[0][0]=5, j=1 → matrix[0][1]=6, j=2 → matrix[0][2]=7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sum Elements in a 1D Array
ladders.desc: Compute the total sum of all numbers in a one-dimensional array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves adding together all elements in a list or array. The intuition is simple: start with an accumulator variable initialized to 0, then iterate through each element in the array, adding its value to the accumulator. The algorithm uses a single loop that goes from the first index to the last index of the array. For each element arr[i], we perform sum += arr[i]. After the loop, sum contains the total. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the array length. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block of 2D array iteration because summing a 2D region essentially reduces to summing multiple 1D rows or columns. In the brute force approach, this operation is extended to two dimensions by nesting loops. Common mistakes include not initializing the sum variable (leading to undefined behavior), using the wrong loop bounds, or modifying the array during summation. This operation is a prerequisite for more complex aggregations and can be practiced independently with various arrays.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn how arrays store elements and access them using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for working with data structures like matrices. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. In most programming languages, arrays are 0-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For example, an array arr = [10, 20, 30] has arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that indices act as addresses to retrieve values. Key operations include accessing an element (using arr[i]), modifying an element (arr[i] = new_value), and iterating through all elements (using a loop with index i from 0 to length-1). Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity for the array is O(n). This feeds into the basic operation of summing elements because summation requires accessing each element via its index. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing incorrectly, accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] for a 3-element array), and confusing the index with the value. This foundation is essential for beginners and can be practiced with simple tasks like printing array elements or finding the first element.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. Accessing index 1 gives value 10.
Original: [100, 200]. After arr[0] = 50, array is [50, 200].

approaches.name: Prefix Sum O(n^2 m^2)
approaches.summary: Precompute a 2D prefix sum array to allow O(1) sum queries for any subrectangle. Then iterate over all subrectangles to find the maximum sum efficiently. Use it when brute force is too slow but matrix dimensions are moderate.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Rectangle in 2D Matrix using Prefix Sum
ladders.desc: Find the maximum sum subrectangle in a 2D matrix using prefix sums for O(1) sum queries.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the subrectangle with the maximum sum in a 2D matrix. The brute-force approach would be O(n²m²) by checking all subrectangles, but computing each sum from scratch would make it O(n³m³). The key optimization is using a 2D prefix sum array to compute any subrectangle sum in O(1) time. First, build a prefix sum matrix where prefix[i][j] stores the sum of all elements from (0,0) to (i-1,j-1). This allows computing the sum of any rectangle from (r1,c1) to (r2,c2) as prefix[r2+1][c2+1] - prefix[r1][c2+1] - prefix[r2+1][c1] + prefix[r1][c1]. Then, iterate over all possible pairs of rows (top and bottom) and columns (left and right) to define subrectangles, compute their sums using the prefix formula, and track the maximum. The intuition is that by precomputing cumulative sums, we avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm runs in O(n²m²) time and O(nm) space for the prefix matrix. This approach is efficient for moderate-sized matrices where n and m are up to about 100. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in prefix indices and incorrect rectangle sum formula. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the prefix sum building and querying into the complete solution.

examples.trace: Matrix: [[1,2,-1],[-3,0,4],[2,-5,3]]. Build prefix sum: [[0,0,0,0],[0,1,3,2],[0,-2,0,6],[0,0,-5,4]]. Iterate all subrectangles: For top=0,bottom=0,left=0,right=0: sum=1; top=0,bottom=0,left=0,right=1: sum=3; top=0,bottom=0,left=0,right=2: sum=2; top=0,bottom=1,left=0,right=0: sum=-2; top=0,bottom=1,left=0,right=1: sum=0; top=0,bottom=1,left=0,right=2: sum=6; top=0,bottom=2,left=0,right=0: sum=0; top=0,bottom=2,left=0,right=1: sum=-5; top=0,bottom=2,left=0,right=2: sum=4; top=1,bottom=1,left=0,right=0: sum=-3; top=1,bottom=1,left=0,right=1: sum=-3; top=1,bottom=1,left=0,right=2: sum=1; top=1,bottom=2,left=0,right=0: sum=-1; top=1,bottom=2,left=0,right=1: sum=-8; top=1,bottom=2,left=0,right=2: sum=2; top=2,bottom=2,left=0,right=0: sum=2; top=2,bottom=2,left=0,right=1: sum=-3; top=2,bottom=2,left=0,right=2: sum=0; also check other combos like top=0,bottom=1,left=1,right=2: sum=prefix[2][3]-prefix[0][3]-prefix[2][1]+prefix[0][1]=6-2-0+3=7 (max). Maximum sum is 7 from rectangle (0,1) to (1,2) with elements [2,-1,0,4].

Matrix: [[-1,-2],[-3,-4]]. Build prefix sum: [[0,0,0],[0,-1,-3],[0,-4,-10]]. Iterate: top=0,bottom=0,left=0,right=0: sum=-1; top=0,bottom=0,left=0,right=1: sum=-3; top=0,bottom=1,left=0,right=0: sum=-4; top=0,bottom=1,left=0,right=1: sum=-10; top=1,bottom=1,left=0,right=0: sum=-3; top=1,bottom=1,left=0,right=1: sum=-7; also check top=0,bottom=0,left=1,right=1: sum=-2; top=1,bottom=1,left=1,right=1: sum=-4. Maximum sum is -1 from single element at (0,0).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Subrectangle Sum using 2D Prefix Sum
ladders.desc: Given a 2D prefix sum array, compute the sum of any subrectangle in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the maximum sum rectangle solution. After building a 2D prefix sum matrix, we need to efficiently query the sum of any rectangle defined by its top-left (r1,c1) and bottom-right (r2,c2) corners. The formula is: sum = prefix[r2+1][c2+1] - prefix[r1][c2+1] - prefix[r2+1][c1] + prefix[r1][c1]. The intuition comes from inclusion-exclusion principle: prefix[r2+1][c2+1] gives sum from (0,0) to (r2,c2). We subtract prefix[r1][c2+1] (sum from (0,0) to (r1-1,c2)) and prefix[r2+1][c1] (sum from (0,0) to (r2,c1-1)), but this double-subtracts the region from (0,0) to (r1-1,c1-1), so we add back prefix[r1][c1]. This allows O(1) queries instead of O(nm) per rectangle. The algorithm: input prefix matrix (size (n+1)x(m+1) with first row/col zeros), r1,c1,r2,c2 indices (0-based). Output the sum. Time O(1), space O(1). Common mistakes: using wrong indices (e.g., forgetting +1 offset) or incorrect formula order. This feeds into L0 by enabling fast iteration over all rectangles. Real code pattern: def query_sum(prefix, r1, c1, r2, c2): return prefix[r2+1][c2+1] - prefix[r1][c2+1] - prefix[r2+1][c1] + prefix[r1][c1].

examples.trace: Given prefix matrix for 3x3 original. Compute sum for rectangle from (0,1) to (1,2): prefix[r2+1][c2+1]=prefix[2][3]=6, prefix[r1][c2+1]=prefix[0][3]=0, prefix[r2+1][c1]=prefix[2][1]=0, prefix[r1][c1]=prefix[0][1]=0. Sum = 6 - 0 - 0 + 0 = 6? Wait, check: Actually prefix indices: r1=0,c1=1,r2=1,c2=2. prefix[r2+1][c2+1]=prefix[2][3]=6, prefix[r1][c2+1]=prefix[0][3]=0, prefix[r2+1][c1]=prefix[2][1]=0, prefix[r1][c1]=prefix[0][1]=0. But expected sum from elements [2,-1,0,4] is 5? Let's recalc: Original matrix: row0: [1,2,-1], row1: [-3,0,4]. Rectangle (0,1) to (1,2): elements 2,-1,0,4 sum=5. Prefix formula error: prefix built as described earlier: [[0,0,0,0],[0,1,3,2],[0,-2,0,6],[0,0,-5,4]]. For r1=0,c1=1,r2=1,c2=2: prefix[2][3]=6, prefix[0][3]=0, prefix[2][1]=0, prefix[0][1]=0, sum=6. But actual sum is 5. Mist: prefix indices: r2+1=2, c2+1=3 correct. But need to verify prefix values: prefix[2][3] corresponds to sum from (0,0) to (1,2): elements 1+2-1-3+0+4=3, not 6. Let's rebuild prefix correctly: For original matrix 3x3, prefix size 4x4. prefix[1][1]=mat[0][0]=1; prefix[1][2]=1+2=3; prefix[1][3]=3+(-1)=2; prefix[2][1]=1+(-3)=-2; prefix[2][2]=1+2-3+0=0; prefix[2][3]=0+4=4? Wait, compute stepwise: prefix[i][j] = mat[i-1][j-1] + prefix[i-1][j] + prefix[i][j-1] - prefix[i-1][j-1]. So prefix[2][3] = mat[1][2]=4 + prefix[1][3]=2 + prefix[2][2]=0 - prefix[1][2]=3 = 4+2+0-3=3. Yes, so correct prefix: [[0,0,0,0],[0,1,3,2],[0,-2,0,3],[0,0,-5,1]]. Then query: prefix[2][3]=3, prefix[0][3]=0, prefix[2][1]=0, prefix[0][1]=0, sum=3. Still not 5. Error in example trace; let's use a simpler correct example. Instead, use matrix [[1,2],[3,4]], prefix=[[0,0,0],[0,1,3],[0,4,10]]. Query r1=0,c1=0,r2=1,c2=1: prefix[2][2]=10, prefix[0][2]=0, prefix[2][0]=0, prefix[0][0]=0, sum=10 correct.

Prefix for 2x2 matrix [[1,2],[3,4]]. Query rectangle from (0,0) to (0,1): prefix[r2+1][c2+1]=prefix[1][2]=3, prefix[r1][c2+1]=prefix[0][2]=0, prefix[r2+1][c1]=prefix[1][0]=0, prefix[r1][c1]=prefix[0][0]=0. Sum = 3 - 0 - 0 + 0 = 3, which matches sum of first row [1,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Build 2D Prefix Sum Array
ladders.desc: Construct a 2D prefix sum matrix from a given 2D matrix to enable fast range sum queries.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is building a 2D prefix sum array, which is a precomputation technique to answer subrectangle sum queries in O(1) time. Given an n x m matrix, we create a (n+1) x (m+1) prefix matrix where prefix[i][j] represents the sum of all elements from the original matrix's top-left corner (0,0) to position (i-1, j-1). The first row and column are initialized to 0 to handle boundaries easily. The intuition is cumulative addition: each prefix[i][j] can be computed using the recurrence: prefix[i][j] = matrix[i-1][j-1] + prefix[i-1][j] + prefix[i][j-1] - prefix[i-1][j-1]. This works because prefix[i-1][j] gives sum above, prefix[i][j-1] gives sum to the left, and we subtract prefix[i-1][j-1] since it's added twice. The algorithm: initialize prefix with zeros of size (n+1)x(m+1). For i from 1 to n, for j from 1 to m, compute prefix[i][j] as above. Time O(nm), space O(nm). Common mistakes: incorrect indices (off-by-one) or misunderstanding the recurrence. This feeds into L1 by providing the prefix array for queries. Real code pattern: def build_prefix(matrix): n,m = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]); prefix = [[0]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(1,n+1): for j in range(1,m+1): prefix[i][j] = matrix[i-1][j-1] + prefix[i-1][j] + prefix[i][j-1] - prefix[i-1][j-1]; return prefix.

examples.trace: Matrix 2x2. Initialize prefix 3x3 with zeros. i=1,j=1: prefix[1][1] = mat[0][0]=1 + prefix[0][1]=0 + prefix[1][0]=0 - prefix[0][0]=0 = 1. i=1,j=2: prefix[1][2] = mat[0][1]=2 + prefix[0][2]=0 + prefix[1][1]=1 - prefix[0][1]=0 = 3. i=2,j=1: prefix[2][1] = mat[1][0]=3 + prefix[1][1]=1 + prefix[2][0]=0 - prefix[1][0]=0 = 4. i=2,j=2: prefix[2][2] = mat[1][1]=4 + prefix[1][2]=3 + prefix[2][1]=4 - prefix[1][1]=1 = 10. Result prefix matrix.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Sum of a 1D Subarray using Prefix Sum
ladders.desc: Use a 1D prefix sum array to compute the sum of any subarray in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: This building block extends the concept of prefix sums to 1D arrays, which is foundational for the 2D version. Given an array of numbers, we precompute a prefix sum array where prefix[i] stores the sum of the first i elements (with prefix[0]=0). Then, the sum of any subarray from index l to r (inclusive) can be computed as prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. The intuition is that prefix[r+1] gives the total sum up to r, and subtracting prefix[l] removes the sum before l. This reduces query time from O(n) to O(1). The algorithm: build prefix array of size n+1: prefix[0]=0; for i from 1 to n: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i-1]. To query sum from l to r: return prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. Time O(n) to build, O(1) per query, space O(n). Common mistakes: incorrect indices (e.g., using r instead of r+1) or not initializing prefix[0]=0. This feeds into L2 as the 1D analog that generalizes to 2D. Real code pattern: def build_prefix_1d(arr): n=len(arr); prefix=[0]*(n+1); for i in range(1,n+1): prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+arr[i-1]; return prefix. def query_1d(prefix,l,r): return prefix[r+1]-prefix[l].

examples.trace: Build prefix: prefix[0]=0; i=1: prefix[1]=0+1=1; i=2: prefix[2]=1+2=3; i=3: prefix[3]=3+3=6; i=4: prefix[4]=6+4=10. Query l=0,r=2: prefix[r+1]=prefix[3]=6, prefix[l]=prefix[0]=0, sum=6-0=6, which matches 1+2+3.

Build prefix: prefix[0]=0; i=1: prefix[1]=0+(-1)=-1; i=2: prefix[2]=-1+2=1; i=3: prefix[3]=1+(-3)=-2; i=4: prefix[4]=-2+4=2. Query l=1,r=3: prefix[r+1]=prefix[4]=2, prefix[l]=prefix[1]=-1, sum=2-(-1)=3, which matches 2+(-3)+4=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Subarrays in an Array
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays of a 1D array by iterating over start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves enumerating all contiguous subarrays of a given array. For an array of length n, there are n*(n+1)/2 subarrays. The intuition is to consider every possible starting index i (from 0 to n-1) and every possible ending index j (from i to n-1), which defines the subarray from i to j inclusive. This is a fundamental step in many algorithms that need to examine all subarrays, such as finding maximum sum subarray (Kadane's algorithm) or, in 2D, extending to subrectangles. The algorithm: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): process subarray arr[i:j+1]. Time O(n²) to generate all subarrays. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop ranges or misunderstanding contiguous vs. non-contiguous. This feeds into L3 as the iteration pattern used when querying sums for all subarrays. Real code pattern: def all_subarrays(arr): n=len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): sub = arr[i:j+1]; # process sub.

examples.trace: Array length 2. i=0: j=0 -> [-1]; j=1 -> [-1,0]. i=1: j=1 -> [0]. Total 3 subarrays.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sum of Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Compute the total sum of all elements in a 1D array by iterating and accumulating.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: calculating the sum of elements in an array. Given an array of numbers, we iterate through each element and add it to a running total. The intuition is straightforward accumulation: start with sum=0, for each element in the array, add it to sum. This foundational operation is used in building prefix sums and many other algorithms. The algorithm: initialize sum=0; for each element x in array: sum += x; return sum. Time O(n) where n is array length, space O(1). Common mistakes: not initializing sum to 0 or using wrong loop bounds. This feeds into L4 as the basic computation needed when processing subarrays. Real code pattern: def array_sum(arr): total=0; for num in arr: total+=num; return total.

examples.trace: Start sum=0. Element 1: sum=0+1=1. Element 2: sum=1+2=3. Element 3: sum=3+3=6. Element 4: sum=6+4=10. Final sum 10.
Start sum=0. Element -1: sum=0+(-1)=-1. Element 0: sum=-1+0=-1. Element 5: sum=-1+5=4. Final sum 4.

approaches.name: Kadane 2D DP O(n^2 m)
approaches.summary: Fix top and bottom rows, compress columns into a 1D array by summing rows, and apply Kadane's algorithm to find maximum subarray sum in 1D. This reduces the 2D problem to repeated 1D problems. Use it for optimal performance in typical scenarios.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Rectangle in 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Find the maximum sum rectangle in a 2D matrix using Kadane's algorithm extended to 2D.
ladders.explanation: The Kadane 2D approach transforms the 2D maximum sum rectangle problem into multiple 1D maximum subarray problems. The key insight is that any rectangle can be defined by its top and bottom rows. By fixing these rows, we can compress the columns between them into a 1D array where each element represents the sum of values in that column from the top to bottom row. This compression reduces the problem to finding the maximum subarray sum in this 1D array using Kadane's algorithm. We iterate over all possible pairs of top and bottom rows, apply compression and Kadane's algorithm, and track the maximum sum found. The intuition is that by considering all vertical ranges, we cover all possible rectangles, and by applying Kadane's algorithm horizontally, we find the best horizontal range for each vertical range. The algorithm runs in O(n²m) time where n is the number of rows and m is the number of columns, with O(m) space for the compressed array. This approach is optimal for typical scenarios where n and m are comparable. Common mistakes include not initializing variables correctly, especially when all numbers are negative, and not handling edge cases like single-row or single-column matrices. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the column compression and Kadane's algorithm subroutines into the complete solution. Real code pattern: Initialize max_sum to negative infinity. For each top row from 0 to n-1: create an array temp of size m initialized to 0. For each bottom row from top to n-1: add each column's value to temp, apply Kadane's algorithm on temp to find max subarray sum, update max_sum if larger. Return max_sum.

examples.trace: Matrix: [[1,2,-1],[-3,4,2],[5,-2,3]]. Initialize max_sum = -inf. Top=0: temp=[0,0,0]. Bottom=0: temp=[1,2,-1], Kadane gives max=3 (subarray [1,2]), max_sum=3. Bottom=1: temp=[-2,6,1], Kadane gives max=7 (subarray [6,1]), max_sum=7. Bottom=2: temp=[3,4,3], Kadane gives max=10 (subarray [3,4,3]), max_sum=10. Top=1: temp=[0,0,0]. Bottom=1: temp=[-3,4,2], Kadane gives max=6 (subarray [4,2]), max_sum=10. Bottom=2: temp=[2,2,5], Kadane gives max=9 (subarray [2,2,5]), max_sum=10. Top=2: temp=[0,0,0]. Bottom=2: temp=[5,-2,3], Kadane gives max=6 (subarray [5] or [3]), max_sum=10. Final max_sum=10, but recheck: For top=0, bottom=2, temp=[3,4,3], Kadane max=10. However, rectangle from (0,0) to (2,2) sum=1+2-1-3+4+2+5-2+3=11? Wait, compute: Row0:1+2-1=2, Row1:-3+4+2=3, Row2:5-2+3=6, total=11. Kadane on [3,4,3]: max subarray sum is 3+4+3=10, not 11. Error: Compression: For top=0, bottom=2, column sums: col0=1-3+5=3, col1=2+4-2=4, col2=-1+2+3=4, so temp=[3,4,4]. Kadane gives max=11 (subarray [3,4,4]), max_sum=11. Top=0, bottom=1: col0=1-3=-2, col1=2+4=6, col2=-1+2=1, temp=[-2,6,1], Kadane max=7. Top=1, bottom=2: col0=-3+5=2, col1=4-2=2, col2=2+3=5, temp=[2,2,5], Kadane max=9. So max_sum=11. Output 11.

Matrix: [[-1,-2],[-3,-4]]. Initialize max_sum = -inf. Top=0: temp=[0,0]. Bottom=0: temp=[-1,-2], Kadane gives max=-1, max_sum=-1. Bottom=1: temp=[-4,-6], Kadane gives max=-4, max_sum=-1. Top=1: temp=[0,0]. Bottom=1: temp=[-3,-4], Kadane gives max=-3, max_sum=-1. Final max_sum=-1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Column Compression and Kadane's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Compress columns between two rows into a 1D array and find maximum subarray sum using Kadane's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the Kadane 2D approach. Given a 2D matrix, a top row index, and a bottom row index, we compress the columns by summing the values in each column from the top to bottom row inclusive. This creates a 1D array where each element represents the cumulative sum of a column over the specified row range. Then, we apply Kadane's algorithm to this compressed array to find the maximum sum contiguous subarray. The intuition is that this subarray corresponds to the best horizontal range for the fixed vertical range defined by the top and bottom rows. The compression step effectively reduces the 2D problem to 1D, allowing us to leverage the efficient O(m) Kadane's algorithm. The algorithm: Initialize an array temp of size m (number of columns) with zeros. For each column j, sum matrix[i][j] for i from top to bottom and store in temp[j]. Then apply Kadane's algorithm on temp to find max sum. Time complexity is O(m) for compression plus O(m) for Kadane, so O(m) total per pair of rows. Space is O(m) for temp. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called for every pair of top and bottom rows in the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing when summing rows or not resetting temp for each new top row. Real code pattern: Function compress_and_kadane(matrix, top, bottom): temp = [0]*m; for j in range(m): for i in range(top, bottom+1): temp[j] += matrix[i][j]; return kadane(temp).

examples.trace: Matrix 3x3, top=0, bottom=2. Initialize temp=[0,0,0]. Column 0: sum rows 0 to 2: 1 + (-3) + 5 = 3. Column 1: 2 + 4 + (-2) = 4. Column 2: (-1) + 2 + 3 = 4. So temp=[3,4,4]. Apply Kadane: current_sum=0, max_sum=-inf. j=0: current_sum=3, max_sum=3. j=1: current_sum=7, max_sum=7. j=2: current_sum=11, max_sum=11. Output 11.

Matrix 2x2, top=1, bottom=1. Initialize temp=[0,0]. Column 0: row1 only: -3. Column 1: row1 only: -4. So temp=[-3,-4]. Apply Kadane: current_sum=0, max_sum=-inf. j=0: current_sum=-3, max_sum=-3. j=1: current_sum=-7, max_sum=-3. Output -3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Kadane's Algorithm for Maximum Subarray Sum
ladders.desc: Find the maximum sum contiguous subarray in a 1D array using Kadane's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: Kadane's algorithm efficiently solves the maximum subarray sum problem in O(n) time. The intuition is to iterate through the array, maintaining a current sum that represents the sum of the subarray ending at the current position. At each element, we decide whether to start a new subarray at this element or extend the previous subarray. Specifically, we compare the current element alone with the sum of the current element plus the previous current sum, and take the maximum. This greedy choice ensures that we always have the best subarray ending at each position. We also keep track of the global maximum sum seen so far. The algorithm works even with negative numbers because starting a new subarray at a negative number might be better if the previous sum is more negative. Steps: Initialize max_sum to negative infinity and current_sum to 0. For each element in the array: update current_sum = max(element, current_sum + element); update max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum). Return max_sum. Time complexity is O(n) and space is O(1). Connection to L1: This algorithm is applied to the compressed column array to find the best horizontal range. Common mistakes include not initializing max_sum properly for all-negative arrays or not resetting current_sum correctly. Real code pattern: Function kadane(arr): max_sum = -inf; current_sum = 0; for num in arr: current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num); max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum); return max_sum.

examples.trace: Initialize max_sum = -inf, current_sum = 0. Element 3: current_sum = max(3, 0+3) = 3; max_sum = max(-inf, 3) = 3. Element 4: current_sum = max(4, 3+4) = 7; max_sum = max(3, 7) = 7. Element 4: current_sum = max(4, 7+4) = 11; max_sum = max(7, 11) = 11. Output 11.

Initialize max_sum = -inf, current_sum = 0. Element -3: current_sum = max(-3, 0-3) = -3; max_sum = max(-inf, -3) = -3. Element -4: current_sum = max(-4, -3-4) = -4; max_sum = max(-3, -4) = -3. Output -3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Column-wise Summation in a 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Sum values in each column over a range of rows to create a 1D array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves compressing a 2D matrix into a 1D array by summing the elements column-wise over a specified row range. Given a matrix, a top row index, and a bottom row index, we compute for each column the total sum of elements from the top row to the bottom row inclusive. This operation transforms the 2D data into a 1D representation where each element captures the cumulative vertical sum for that column. The intuition is that this compression allows us to treat the rectangle defined by the row range as a 1D array, simplifying subsequent operations. The algorithm: Initialize an array result of size equal to the number of columns, all zeros. For each column index j, iterate over row indices i from top to bottom, adding matrix[i][j] to result[j]. This runs in O(m * (bottom-top+1)) time, but in the context of the full solution, we optimize by incrementally updating as we move the bottom row. Space is O(m). Connection to L2: This compressed array is the input to Kadane's algorithm. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in row indices or not initializing the result array properly. Real code pattern: Function compress_columns(matrix, top, bottom): m = number of columns; result = [0]*m; for j in range(m): for i in range(top, bottom+1): result[j] += matrix[i][j]; return result.

examples.trace: Matrix 3x3, top=0, bottom=1. Initialize result=[0,0,0]. Column 0: rows 0 and 1: 1 + (-3) = -2. Column 1: 2 + 4 = 6. Column 2: (-1) + 2 = 1. So result=[-2,6,1].
Matrix 2x2, top=0, bottom=0. Initialize result=[0,0]. Column 0: row0 only: -1. Column 1: row0 only: -2. So result=[-1,-2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over All Pairs of Rows
ladders.desc: Generate all possible top and bottom row pairs in a 2D matrix.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating over all combinations of top and bottom row indices in a matrix. For a matrix with n rows, the top row can range from 0 to n-1, and for each top row, the bottom row can range from the top row to n-1. This ensures that we consider all possible vertical ranges (rectangles defined by row intervals). The intuition is that by fixing a top and bottom row, we define a submatrix that includes all rows between them, and then we can process the columns within this submatrix. This iteration is key to covering all possible rectangles in the 2D problem. The algorithm uses nested loops: for top in range(n): for bottom in range(top, n): process the submatrix from top to bottom. The number of pairs is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). Connection to L3: For each pair, we perform column compression. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds or not including the case where top equals bottom (single-row rectangles). Real code pattern: n = number of rows; for top in range(n): for bottom in range(top, n): # Process rows top to bottom.

examples.trace: n=3. Top=0: bottom from 0 to 2: (0,0), (0,1), (0,2). Top=1: bottom from 1 to 2: (1,1), (1,2). Top=2: bottom from 2 to 2: (2,2). Total 6 pairs.
n=2. Top=0: bottom from 0 to 1: (0,0), (0,1). Top=1: bottom from 1 to 1: (1,1). Total 3 pairs.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding 2D Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic operations on 2D arrays: accessing elements, rows, and columns.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of 2D arrays (matrices), which are grids of elements arranged in rows and columns. In programming, a 2D array is often represented as a list of lists, where each inner list corresponds to a row. Elements are accessed using two indices: matrix[i][j] where i is the row index (starting from 0) and j is the column index (starting from 0). The intuition is that a 2D array allows us to store and manipulate data in a tabular form, which is essential for problems involving grids or matrices. Key operations include: iterating over all elements using nested loops, accessing a specific row (matrix[i]), accessing a specific column (requires iterating over rows to get matrix[k][j] for fixed j), and understanding the dimensions (number of rows n and number of columns m). This foundation is crucial for working with the matrix in the maximum sum rectangle problem. Connection to L4: Iterating over row pairs requires understanding how to index rows. Common mistakes include confusing row and column indices or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]]; n = len(matrix); m = len(matrix[0]); to access element at row 0, column 1: value = matrix[0][1] (which is 2).

examples.trace: Matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns. Row index 0: first inner list [1,2,3]. Column index 1: for each row, take element at index 1: row0 gives 2, row1 gives 5, so column is [2,5]. Element at row index 1, column index 2: matrix[1][2] = 6.

Matrix has 3 rows and 1 column. Row index 2: third inner list [1]. Column index 0: for each row, take element at index 0: row0 gives -1, row1 gives 0, row2 gives 1, so column is [-1,0,1]. Element at (0,0): matrix[0][0] = -1.
